---
title: "Machine Learning"
subtitle: "3316712 AI Computational Pharmaceutical Sciences"
author: Natapol Pornputtapong, PhD
institute: Chulalongkorn University
date: today # today | last-modified
date-format: "DD MMM YYYY" # see https://momentjs.com/docs/#/displaying/format/
pdf-engine: xelatex
format:
  rxquarto-revealjs:
    self-contained: false # set to true to produce a single HTML file
    transition: none # none | fade | slide | convex | concave | zoom
    background-transition: none
    # scrollable: true
---

# Part 1: Artificial Intelligence Landscape

## What is Artificial Intelligence?

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Core Capabilities**

- 🧠 **Reasoning** — logical problem solving
- 📚 **Learning** — improving from experience
- 👁️ **Perception** — interpreting sensory input
- 💬 **Natural Language** — understanding text/speech
- 🤖 **Planning** — goal-directed action
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Application Domains**

- Drug discovery & design
- Medical image analysis
- Clinical decision support
- Genomics & proteomics
- Pharmacovigilance
:::
::::

## The AI Hierarchy

::: {.absolute top=100 left=0 width="1000" height="550" style="background: #49243E; border-radius: 20px;"}

:::

::: {.absolute top=120 left=50 style="color: white;"}
AI: Artificial Intelligence
<br /><br />
the science and engineering of making intelligent machines, especially intelligent computer programs

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1 .absolute top=200 left=50 width="950" height="450" style="background: #704264; border-radius: 20px;"}

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1 .absolute top=220 left=100 style="color: white;"}
ML: Machine Learning
<br /><br />
design and train algorithms to learn from and act on data
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=2 .absolute top=300 left=100 width="900" height="350" style="background: #BB8493; border-radius: 20px;"}
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=2 .absolute top=300 left=150 style="color: white;"}
DL: Deep Learning
<br /><br />
multi-layered neural networks, called deep neural networks, to simulate the complex decision-making power of the human brain
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3 .absolute top=380 left=150 width="850" height="270" style="background: #DBAFA0; border-radius: 20px;"}
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3 .absolute top=400 left=200 style="color: white;"}
GenAI: Generative Deep Learning
<br /><br />
a subset of AI used to produce text, images, videos, or others response to specific prompts. ^[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence]
:::

## AI: Rule-Based vs. Learning-Based

| Feature | Traditional AI | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge** | Hard-coded rules | Learned from data |
| **Adaptability** | Manual updates needed | Improves automatically |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Scales with data |
| **Interpretability** | High (explicit rules) | Often lower (black box) |
| **Example** | Expert systems, chess engines | Spam filters, image classifiers |


# Part 2: Machine Learning

## What is Machine Learning?

> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a field of AI that enables systems to **learn and improve from experience** without being explicitly programmed.

Instead of writing rules:

```
IF temperature > 38.5 THEN fever = True
IF fever AND cough THEN risk = High
...
```

We let the algorithm **discover patterns** from labeled examples.

## Machine Learning Key Components

1. **Data** — examples & labels
2. **Model** — mathematical function
3. **Loss function** — how wrong are we?
4. **Optimization** — minimize loss


## Types of Machine Learning

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="33%"}
**Supervised Learning**

- Labeled training data
- Learn input → output mapping
- Examples:
  - Classification
  - Regression
- Applications: diagnosis, prognosis
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="33%"}
**Unsupervised Learning**

- No labels required
- Find hidden structure
- Examples:
  - Clustering
  - Dimensionality reduction
- Applications: patient stratification
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="33%"}
**Reinforcement Learning**

- Agent + environment
- Learn by reward/penalty
- Examples:
  - Game playing
  - Drug dosing optimization
- Applications: clinical decision
:::
::::

---

## Supervised Learning in Detail

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Training Data<br />(X, y)"] --> B["ML Algorithm"]
  B --> C["Learned Model<br />f(X) ≈ y"]
  D["New Data X*"] --> C
  C --> E["Prediction ŷ"]
```

**Classification** — predict discrete categories (e.g., malignant vs. benign)

**Regression** — predict continuous values (e.g., drug concentration, IC50)

---

## Model Properties

- Performance
- Linearity
- Interpretability
- Scalability
- Robustness

## Common ML Algorithms 

::: {.r-fit-text}
| Algorithm | Type | Strengths |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear/Logistic Regression** | Supervised | Interpretable, fast |
| **Decision Trees** | Supervised | Interpretable, non-linear |
| **Random Forest** | Supervised | Robust, handles missing data |
| **Support Vector Machine** | Supervised | Good for high-dim data |
| **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** | Supervised | State-of-art tabular data |
| **K-Means** | Unsupervised | Simple clustering |
| **PCA** | Unsupervised | Dimensionality reduction |
| **Neural Networks** | Both | Universal approximator |
:::

---

## How Machine Learning Works {.scrollable}

```{mermaid}
---
config:
  flowchart:
    nodeSpacing: 50
    rankSpacing: 50
    padding: 5
  look: handDrawn
  theme: forest
  layout: elk
  elk:
    mergeEdges: true
    nodePlacementStrategy: LINEAR_SEGMENTS
---
flowchart TD
  A[Collect Data] --> B[Preprocess Data<br />clean, normalize, encode]
  B --> C[Split Data<br />train, validation, test]
  C --> D[Choose Train Model]
  D --> E[Evaluate Performance]
  E --> F{Good enough}
  F -- No --> G[Tune Hyperparameters<br />/Change Model]
  G --> D
  F -- Yes --> H[Deploy Model]
  H --> I[Monitor Retrain]
```

---

## Key Concepts: Bias-Variance Tradeoff

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}

- Underfitting (High Bias)
  - Model too simple  
  - Poor on training AND test data
  - Fix: more complex model, more features
- Overfitting (High Variance)
  - Model too complex
  - Great on training, poor on test
  - Fix: regularization, more data, simpler model
- Sweet Spot
  - Balances bias & variance
  - Generalizes well to new data
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{mermaid}
xychart-beta
  title "Bias-Variance Tradeoff"
  x-axis ["Simple", "Low", "Medium", "High", "Complex"]
  y-axis "Error" 0 --> 10
  line [9, 6, 3.5, 2.5, 2]
  line [9, 5, 3.5, 5, 8]
```

::: {.r-fit-text style="font-size:0.65em; text-align:center;"}
📉 **Train Error** (solid) decreases with complexity  
📈 **Test Error** (line) — U-shaped — optimal at middle  
⬆️ **Left** = High Bias (Underfitting)  
⬆️ **Right** = High Variance (Overfitting)
:::
:::
::::

---

## Classification Evaluation Metrics 

| Metric | Formula | Use When |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | (TP+TN)/Total | Balanced classes |
| Precision | TP/(TP+FP) | Cost of false positives high |
| Recall | TP/(TP+FN) | Cost of false negatives high |
| F1 Score | 2·P·R/(P+R) | Imbalanced classes |
| AUC-ROC | Area under ROC | Overall discriminability |

## Regression Evaluation Metrics 

| Metric | Formula |
|---|---|
| MAE | mean(|y - ŷ|) |
| RMSE | sqrt(mean((y - ŷ)²)) |
| R² | 1 - SS_res/SS_tot |


## Cross-Validation

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    F1[Fold 1 Test, Train, Train, Train, Train]
    F2[Fold 2 Train, Test, Train, Train, Train]
    F3[Fold 3 Train, Train, Test, Train, Train]
    F4[Fold 4 Train, Train, Train, Test, Train]
    F5[Fold 5 Train, Train, Train, Train, Test]
  F1 & F2 & F3 & F4 & F5 --> Avg[Average Score = CV Score]
```

- More reliable estimate of model performance
- Reduces overfitting risk during model selection


# Part 3: Deep Learning

## What is Deep Learning?

**Deep Learning (DL)** uses artificial **neural networks** with many layers to learn hierarchical representations from raw data.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Why "Deep"?**

- Multiple hidden layers
- Each layer learns more abstract features
- Pixels → Edges → Shapes → Objects

**Key Advantage**

- **Automatic feature extraction** — no manual feature engineering needed
- Excels at **images, text, audio, sequences**
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
```
Input Layer   Hidden Layers   Output Layer
   [x₁] ──┐
   [x₂] ──┤─→[●][●][●]─→[●][●]─→[ŷ]
   [x₃] ──┤─→[●][●][●]─→[●][●]
   [x₄] ──┘
   
   Each ● = neuron (weighted sum + activation)
```
:::
::::


## Neural Network Components

::: {style="font-size:0.65em; "}
**Neuron (Perceptron):**
$$z = \sum_{i} w_i x_i + b, \quad \hat{y} = \sigma(z)$$

**Activation Functions:**

| Function | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ) | Binary output |
| ReLU | max(0, z) | Hidden layers (CNN) |
| Softmax | eᶻⁱ/Σeᶻʲ | Multi-class output |
| Tanh | (eᶻ-e⁻ᶻ)/(eᶻ+e⁻ᶻ) | RNN hidden states |

**Training:** Backpropagation + Gradient Descent

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$$
:::

## Deep Learning Architectures

::: {style="font-size:0.8em; "}
| Architecture | Key Feature | Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedforward (FNN/MLP)** | Fully connected layers | Tabular data classification |
| **Convolutional (CNN)** | Spatial feature extraction | Medical imaging, histopathology |
| **Recurrent (RNN/LSTM)** | Sequential/temporal data | EHR time series, text |
| **Transformer** | Attention mechanism | Drug-protein interaction, NLP |
| **Autoencoder** | Unsupervised representation | Anomaly detection, de-noising |
| **GAN** | Generative adversarial | Molecular generation |
:::

## Deep Learning in Pharmaceutical Sciences

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Drug Discovery**

- AlphaFold2 — protein structure prediction
- Molecular property prediction (QSAR)
- De novo drug design (generative models)
- ADMET prediction

**Clinical Applications**

- Pathology image analysis (H&E, IHC)
- Radiology image segmentation
- Drug-drug interaction prediction
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Genomics & Omics**

- Variant calling from sequencing
- Gene expression prediction
- Biomarker discovery
- Multi-omics data integration

**Pharmacovigilance**

- Adverse event detection from text
- Social media mining
- Signal detection from claims data
:::
::::

# Part 4: Machine Learning with Julia & SciML

## Why Julia for Machine Learning?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**Performance**

- C-level speed with Python-like syntax
- JIT compilation via LLVM
- No need to vectorize — loops are fast
- Ideal for large numerical computations

**Scientific Computing**

- Native array operations
- Excellent linear algebra support
- Symbolic math (Symbolics.jl)
- Automatic differentiation (Zygote.jl)
:::
::: {.column .r-fit-text width="50%"}
**SciML Ecosystem**

```
SciML Organization
├── Flux.jl          # Deep Learning
├── MLJ.jl           # ML framework
├── DataFrames.jl    # Data manipulation
├── GLM.jl           # Linear/GLM models
├── DecisionTree.jl  # Trees, Random Forest
├── LIBSVM.jl        # SVM
└── Statistics.jl    # Core statistics
```
:::
::::

## Setting Up the Environment

```julia
# Install required packages (run once)
using Pkg
Pkg.add([
    "MLJ",
    "MLJDecisionTreeInterface",  # Decision Trees
    "XGBoost",                   # Gradient Boosting
    "CSV",                       # Read CSV files
    "DataFrames",                # Data manipulation
    "Plots",                     # Visualization
    "StatsPlots",                # Statistical plots
    "CategoricalArrays"          # Categorical variables
])
```

```julia
# Load packages
using MLJ, CSV, DataFrames, CategoricalArrays
using Plots, StatsPlots
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Julia packages are compiled on first use — subsequent runs are much faster.
:::

## MLJ.jl: The Unified ML Framework

**MLJ** (Machine Learning in Julia) provides a unified interface to 150+ ML algorithms.

```julia
using MLJ

# Load a model
Tree = @load DecisionTreeClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Instantiate with hyperparameters
tree = Tree(max_depth=5)

# Wrap data in MLJ format
X, y = ... # features and target
mach = machine(tree, X, y)

# Train
fit!(mach, rows=train)

# Predict
ŷ = predict(mach, rows=test)

# Evaluate
evaluate!(mach, resampling=CV(nfolds=5), measure=accuracy)
```

## The Titanic Dataset

[Kaggle Titanic Dataset](titanic.csv)

**Objective:** Predict whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster.

**This is a Binary Classification Problem:**
- Target: `Survived` (0 = No, 1 = Yes)

::: {style="font-size:0.65em; "}
| Feature | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `Pclass` | Ordinal | Passenger class (1, 2, 3) |
| `Sex` | Categorical | male / female |
| `Age` | Continuous | Age in years |
| `SibSp` | Integer | # siblings/spouses aboard |
| `Parch` | Integer | # parents/children aboard |
| `Fare` | Continuous | Ticket fare |
| `Embarked` | Categorical | Port (C, Q, S) |
:::

## Step 1: Load and Inspect Data

```julia
using CSV, DataFrames

# Load data
df = CSV.read("titanic.csv", DataFrame)

# Inspect
println("Shape: ", size(df))
println("\nFirst 5 rows:")
first(df, 5)
```

```julia
# Basic statistics
describe(df, :all)
```

```julia
# Check missing values
missing_count = combine(df, names(df) .=> (x -> sum(ismissing.(x))) .=> names(df))
println(missing_count)
```

::: {.callout-note}
The Titanic dataset has missing values in `Age` (~20%), `Cabin` (~77%), and `Embarked` (~0.2%).
:::

---

## Step 2: Exploratory Data Analysis

```julia
using StatsPlots

# Survival by gender
@df df groupedbar(:Sex, :Survived, 
    group=:Survived,
    xlabel="Sex", ylabel="Count",
    title="Survival by Gender",
    labels=["Died" "Survived"],
    color=[:salmon :steelblue])
```

```julia
# Age distribution by survival
@df df violin(:Survived, :Age, 
    group=:Survived,
    xlabel="Survived", ylabel="Age",
    title="Age Distribution by Survival",
    labels=["Died" "Survived"])
```

```julia
# Survival rate by passenger class
survival_by_class = combine(groupby(df, :Pclass),
    :Survived => mean => :survival_rate)
println(survival_by_class)
```

---

## Step 3: Data Preprocessing

```julia
using DataFrames, CategoricalArrays, Statistics

df_clean = copy(df)

# 1. Drop irrelevant columns
select!(df_clean, Not([:PassengerId, :Name, :Ticket, :Cabin]))

# 2. Impute missing Age with median
age_median = median(skipmissing(df_clean.Age))
df_clean.Age = coalesce.(df_clean.Age, age_median)

# 3. Impute missing Embarked with mode
df_clean.Embarked = coalesce.(df_clean.Embarked, "S")

# 4. Encode Sex: female=1, male=0
df_clean.Sex = ifelse.(df_clean.Sex .== "female", 1, 0)

# 5. One-hot encode Embarked
df_clean.Embarked_C = Int.(df_clean.Embarked .== "C")
df_clean.Embarked_Q = Int.(df_clean.Embarked .== "Q")
select!(df_clean, Not(:Embarked))

# 6. Feature engineering
df_clean.FamilySize = df_clean.SibSp .+ df_clean.Parch .+ 1
df_clean.IsAlone = Int.(df_clean.FamilySize .== 1)

println("Processed shape: ", size(df_clean))
```

## Step 4: Prepare MLJ-Compatible Data

```julia
using MLJ

# Separate features and target
target = :Survived
features = setdiff(names(df_clean), [string(target)])

X = select(df_clean, features)
y = df_clean[:, target]

# Convert target to categorical (required for classification)
y = CategoricalArray(y)

# Coerce feature types for MLJ
X = coerce(X, 
    :Pclass     => OrderedFactor,
    :Sex        => Count,
    :Age        => Continuous,
    :SibSp      => Count,
    :Parch      => Count,
    :Fare       => Continuous,
    :FamilySize => Count,
    :IsAlone    => Count
)

println("X:", schema(X))
println("y:", scitype(y))
```

---

## Step 5: Train/Test Split

```julia
using MLJ

# Reproducible split: 80% train, 20% test
train, test = partition(eachindex(y), 0.8, shuffle=true, rng=42)

println("Training samples: ", length(train))
println("Test samples:     ", length(test))
println("Survival rate (train): ", 
    round(mean(y[train] .== "1") * 100, digits=1), "%")
println("Survival rate (test):  ", 
    round(mean(y[test] .== "1") * 100, digits=1), "%")
```

## Step 6: Train a Decision Tree

```julia
using MLJ
MLJ.@load DecisionTreeClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Define model
tree_clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5)

# Wrap in machine (binds model + data)
mach_tree = machine(tree_clf, X, y)

# Train on training set
fit!(mach_tree, rows=train, verbosity=0)

# Predict on test set
ŷ_tree = predict_mode(mach_tree, rows=test)

# Evaluate
acc_tree = accuracy(ŷ_tree, y[test])
println("Decision Tree Accuracy: ", round(acc_tree * 100, digits=2), "%")
```

## Step 7: Train a Random Forest

```julia
MLJ.@load RandomForestClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Define model with hyperparameters
rf_clf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_trees   = 100,
    max_depth = 10,
    min_samples_leaf = 5,
    n_subfeatures = 3
)

mach_rf = machine(rf_clf, X, y)
fit!(mach_rf, rows=train, verbosity=0)
ŷ_rf = predict_mode(mach_rf, rows=test)

acc_rf = accuracy(ŷ_rf, y[test])
println("Random Forest Accuracy: ", round(acc_rf * 100, digits=2), "%")
```

## Step 8: Cross-Validation Evaluation

```julia
using MLJ

# 5-fold cross-validation
cv = CV(nfolds=5, shuffle=true, rng=42)

# Evaluate multiple metrics at once
eval_rf = evaluate(rf_clf, X, y,
    resampling = cv,
    measures   = [accuracy, f1score, auc],
    verbosity  = 0
)

println("\n=== Random Forest Cross-Validation ===")
for (m, v) in zip(eval_rf.measure, eval_rf.measurement)
    println("$(m):  ", round(v, digits=4))
end
println("Per-fold accuracy: ", round.(eval_rf.per_fold[1], digits=3))
```

## Step 9: Hyperparameter Tuning

```julia
using MLJ

# Define search range
r1 = range(rf_clf, :n_trees,      lower=50,  upper=300, scale=:linear)
r2 = range(rf_clf, :max_depth,    lower=3,   upper=15)
r3 = range(rf_clf, :n_subfeatures, lower=2,  upper=6)

# Grid search with cross-validation
tuned_rf = TunedModel(
    model     = rf_clf,
    resampling = CV(nfolds=5, rng=42),
    tuning    = Grid(resolution=5),
    range     = [r1, r2, r3],
    measure   = accuracy
)

mach_tuned = machine(tuned_rf, X, y)
fit!(mach_tuned, rows=train, verbosity=0)

# Best model
best_model = fitted_params(mach_tuned).best_model
println("Best parameters:")
println("  n_trees:       ", best_model.n_trees)
println("  max_depth:     ", best_model.max_depth)
println("  n_subfeatures: ", best_model.n_subfeatures)
```

## Step 10: Feature Importance

```julia
# Get feature importances from trained Random Forest
fp = fitted_params(mach_rf)
importances = fp.forest.feature_importances

# Sort and display
feat_names = names(X)
imp_df = sort(
    DataFrame(feature=feat_names, importance=importances),
    :importance, rev=true
)
println(imp_df)

# Plot
bar(imp_df.feature, imp_df.importance,
    xlabel="Feature",
    ylabel="Importance",
    title="Random Forest Feature Importance (Titanic)",
    xrotation=30,
    legend=false,
    color=:steelblue)
```

## Step 11: Model Comparison

```julia
using MLJ

# Load all model types
MLJ.@load LogisticClassifier pkg=MLJLinearModels
MLJ.@load XGBoostClassifier  pkg=XGBoost

models = [
    ("Logistic Regression", LogisticClassifier()),
    ("Decision Tree",       DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5)),
    ("Random Forest",       RandomForestClassifier(n_trees=100)),
    ("XGBoost",             XGBoostClassifier(num_round=100)),
]

results = []
for (name, model) in models
    ev = evaluate(model, X, y,
        resampling = CV(nfolds=5, rng=42),
        measures   = [accuracy, f1score],
        verbosity  = 0)
    push!(results, (
        Model    = name,
        Accuracy = round(ev.measurement[1], digits=4),
        F1_Score = round(ev.measurement[2], digits=4)
    ))
end

println(DataFrame(results))
```

## Model Comparison Results

```
Model                  Accuracy   F1_Score
─────────────────────────────────────────
Logistic Regression    0.8045     0.7412
Decision Tree          0.7921     0.7283
Random Forest          0.8247     0.7698
XGBoost                0.8316     0.7801
```

```julia
# Visualize comparison
model_names = [r.Model for r in results]
accuracies  = [r.Accuracy for r in results]

bar(model_names, accuracies,
    title="Model Accuracy Comparison — Titanic",
    xlabel="Model", ylabel="Accuracy (5-fold CV)",
    ylim=(0.7, 0.9), legend=false,
    color=[:steelblue, :salmon, :seagreen, :orchid],
    xrotation=20)
```

## Step 12: Confusion Matrix & ROC Curve

```julia
using MLJ, Plots

# Predictions with probabilities
ŷ_prob = predict(mach_rf, rows=test)

# Confusion matrix
cm = ConfusionMatrix(zero_division=0)
println("Confusion Matrix:")
println(confusion_matrix(predict_mode(mach_rf, rows=test), y[test]))

# ROC Curve
fprs, tprs, thresholds = roc_curve(ŷ_prob, y[test])
auc_val = auc(ŷ_prob, y[test])

plot(fprs, tprs,
    xlabel="False Positive Rate",
    ylabel="True Positive Rate",
    title="ROC Curve — Random Forest (Titanic)",
    label="AUC = $(round(auc_val, digits=3))",
    linewidth=2, color=:steelblue)
plot!([0,1], [0,1], linestyle=:dash, color=:gray, label="Random")
```

## Complete Pipeline: Summary

```julia
using MLJ, CSV, DataFrames, CategoricalArrays

# ── 1. Load
df = CSV.read("titanic.csv", DataFrame)

# ── 2. Preprocess (function defined earlier)
df_clean = preprocess_titanic(df)

# ── 3. Feature/target split
X = select(df_clean, Not(:Survived))
y = CategoricalArray(df_clean.Survived)
X = coerce(X, :Pclass => OrderedFactor, :Age => Continuous, :Fare => Continuous)

# ── 4. Split
train, test = partition(eachindex(y), 0.8, shuffle=true, rng=42)

# ── 5. Train best model
MLJ.@load RandomForestClassifier pkg=DecisionTree
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_trees=100, max_depth=10)
mach  = machine(model, X, y)
fit!(mach, rows=train)

# ── 6. Evaluate
ŷ = predict_mode(mach, rows=test)
println("Final Accuracy: ", round(accuracy(ŷ, y[test]) * 100, digits=2), "%")
```

## Summary

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**AI / ML / DL Concepts**

- AI is the broad field of intelligent machines
- ML learns patterns from data automatically
- DL uses deep neural networks for complex tasks
- Bias-variance tradeoff is fundamental
- Always evaluate with cross-validation

**ML Workflow**

1. Collect & explore data (EDA)
2. Preprocess (clean, encode, engineer features)
3. Split: train / validation / test
4. Train & tune models
5. Evaluate & compare
6. Deploy & monitor
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Julia SciML for ML**

- **MLJ.jl** — unified ML interface
- **DataFrames.jl** — data manipulation
- **CSV.jl** — data loading
- **Plots.jl** — visualization
- Performance of C with readability of Python

**Titanic Results**

| Model | Accuracy |
|---|---|
| Logistic Regression | ~80% |
| Random Forest | ~82% |
| XGBoost | ~83% |
:::
::::

## Further Reading & Resources

**Julia & SciML**

- [MLJ.jl Documentation](https://alan-turing-institute.github.io/MLJ.jl/dev/)
- [SciML Organization](https://sciml.ai/)
- [Julia for Data Science](https://juliadatascience.io/)

**Machine Learning**

- Hastie et al. — *Elements of Statistical Learning* (free PDF)
- Goodfellow et al. — *Deep Learning* (free at deeplearningbook.org)

## References

1. Murphy, K.P. (2012). *Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective*. MIT Press.
2. Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y., & Courville, A. (2016). *Deep Learning*. MIT Press.
3. Blaom, A.D. et al. (2020). MLJ: A Julia package for composable machine learning. *Journal of Open Source Software*, 5(55), 2704.
4. Vamathevan, J. et al. (2019). Applications of machine learning in drug discovery and development. *Nature Reviews Drug Discovery*, 18, 463–477.
5. Kaggle Titanic Dataset: [https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic](https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic)
